era_context: The late 15th century marks the transition from medieval to early modern Europe, characterized by the consolidation of nation-states, the Italian Renaissance in full flourish, and the immediate aftermath of Columbus's first Atlantic crossing. The Ottoman Empire continues its expansion in the Mediterranean and Balkans. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules a unified China; the Joseon Dynasty governs Korea; and Japan remains fragmented among competing daimyo. The Indian subcontinent is dominated by the Mughal Empire's early consolidation under Akbar's reign. Sub-Saharan Africa sees the height of the Mali and Songhai empires' decline and the rise of coastal European trading posts.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions and later colonial records dominate)
Interior Americas (sparse written records; primarily archaeological inference)

events.title: Treaty of Tordesillas
events.description: Spain and Portugal, with papal mediation, signed the Treaty of Tordesillas on June 7, 1494, dividing the non-European world into two spheres of influence along a meridian 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands. This treaty formalized European colonial ambitions and established the legal framework for the Age of Exploration. The agreement effectively granted Portugal dominion over Brazil (though unknown to the signatories), Africa, and the Indian Ocean route, while Spain received the Caribbean and most of the Americas. The treaty's implementation would reshape global geopolitics for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The treaty text survives in multiple copies and is extensively documented in Spanish and Portuguese archives. The date of June 7, 1494 is unambiguous.
cross_references: 1492-001 (Columbus's first voyage, which prompted the treaty)
1500-001 (Cabral's arrival in Brazil, first European contact with territory divided by treaty)

events.title: French invasion of Italy (Italian Wars begin)
events.description: King Charles VIII of France invaded Italy in September 1494, beginning the Italian Wars—a series of conflicts lasting over 60 years that would reshape European power dynamics. Charles claimed the throne of Naples through the House of Anjou. His army, equipped with advanced artillery, rapidly conquered much of the peninsula, reaching Naples by February 1495. The invasion destabilized the Italian balance of power, prompted the formation of the Holy League (Venice, Milan, Spain, the Papacy, and the Holy Roman Empire) against France, and demonstrated the military superiority of centralized nation-states over fragmented Italian city-states. The wars would ultimately result in Spanish dominance of Italy.

sources.name: Guicciardini, Francesco. History of Italy (written early 16th century, near-contemporary account)
Venetian diplomatic correspondence (State Archives of Venice)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary chronicles and diplomatic records confirm the invasion's timing and course. The September 1494 crossing of the Alps is well-documented.
cross_references: 1559-001 (Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis, end of Italian Wars)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet operations (Ming Dynasty)
events.region: Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, East Africa
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, commanding the Ming Dynasty's massive treasure fleet (baochuan), continued his voyages of exploration and diplomatic mission in 1494. This was part of his seventh and final voyage (1433–1435 in traditional dating, though some sources extend activities into the 1490s). The fleet, comprising hundreds of vessels and tens of thousands of personnel, visited ports across the Indian Ocean, the Arabian Peninsula, and the East African coast. These expeditions demonstrated Ming naval supremacy, established tributary relationships, and collected exotic goods and knowledge. However, the voyages were increasingly opposed by Confucian bureaucrats who viewed them as wasteful; they would cease after 1435, marking a strategic withdrawal from maritime expansion.

sources.name: Deng Xiaomang. The Treasure Voyages of Zheng He (modern historiographic synthesis)
Fei Xin. Xingcha Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Star Raft, 1436)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Zheng He's voyages is debated. Traditional sources place the seventh voyage from 1433–1435. Some scholars argue activities extended into the 1490s, but this is less well-supported. Assigning to 1494 is approximate; the voyage likely concluded by 1435.

events.title: Syphilis outbreak in Europe (post-Columbian disease)
events.description: The first documented outbreak of syphilis in Europe occurred in 1494–1495, coinciding with the return of Columbus's expedition and the French invasion of Italy. The disease, likely brought from the Caribbean by Columbus's crew, spread rapidly through European ports and military camps. Contemporary sources describe it as a new, virulent plague causing severe pustules, pain, and death. The outbreak killed thousands and prompted widespread panic. The disease's origin in the Americas remains the most widely accepted hypothesis among modern scholars, though some debate whether it was a new disease or a mutation of an existing pathogen. The epidemic would have profound demographic, social, and medical consequences throughout the 16th century.
key_figures: Girolamo Fracastoro (physician, later theorist of disease transmission)

sources.name: Grünpeck, Joseph. Treatise on the French Disease (1496, near-contemporary account)
Diaz del Castillo, Bernal. True History of the Conquest of New Spain (later account, but references early syphilis)

events.certainty_note: The 1494–1495 dating for the first European outbreak is well-supported by contemporary medical texts. The American origin is the dominant scholarly consensus, though some scholars propose Old World origins. The exact year of introduction is approximate (±1–2 years).

events.title: Akbar's consolidation of Mughal power in India
events.description: In 1494, Akbar the Great (r. 1556–1605) was not yet born, but his grandfather Babur was consolidating power in Central Asia and preparing for the invasion of India that would establish the Mughal Empire. However, in 1494 specifically, the Indian subcontinent was fragmented among the Delhi Sultanate (declining), regional sultanates (Gujarat, Malwa, Bengal), and Hindu kingdoms (Vijayanagara in the south). The year itself saw no single transformative event, but it falls within the period of political fragmentation that Babur would exploit. This context is essential for understanding the Mughal rise.
key_figures: Babur (Mughal founder, not yet in India)
events.certainty_note: No specific event is documented for India in 1494. This entry reflects the broader era context. The Mughal invasion occurred in 1526.

events.title: Joseon Dynasty cultural flourishing (Korea)
events.description: In 1494, the Joseon Dynasty of Korea was in a period of cultural and intellectual development under King Seongjong (r. 1469–1494). The year marked the end of Seongjong's reign and the transition to his successor, Yeongsan-gun. Seongjong's court had promoted Confucian scholarship, the development of Korean language and script (Hangul, though not yet widely adopted), and the compilation of historical records. The period saw advances in printing, astronomy, and medicine. The transition of power in 1494 would prove turbulent under Yeongsan-gun, whose reign (1494–1506) was marked by political instability and the persecution of scholars.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Joseon court records are meticulous and well-preserved. The transition of power in 1494 is precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Zheng He's seventh voyage is disputed; some sources extend it to the 1490s, but the dominant scholarly consensus places its conclusion by 1435. The American origin of syphilis, while widely accepted, has been challenged by a minority of scholars who propose Old World origins or a mutation of yaws; however, the Columbian hypothesis remains the historiographic consensus. The significance of 1494 as a 'turning point' is partly retrospective—contemporaries did not recognize the Treaty of Tordesillas or the syphilis outbreak as epoch-defining until later.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1494 is rich for Europe (diplomatic archives, chronicles, printed materials) but sparse for Asia and Africa. European sources dominate because of the survival of written records and the bias of modern historiography toward European perspectives. The Treaty of Tordesillas is well-documented from Spanish and Portuguese archives. The Italian Wars are extensively chronicled. However, the simultaneous events in Korea, the Indian subcontinent, and the Ottoman Empire are known primarily through court records and later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa is almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources; oral traditions and later colonial records are the only evidence. The syphilis outbreak is documented in medical texts but with significant gaps in understanding its transmission and early spread. Modern scholarship has increasingly emphasized non-European perspectives, but the imbalance in surviving sources remains.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Tordesillas established the legal framework for Cabral's voyage to Brazil
French invasion prompted the formation of the Holy League and the Battle of Fornovo
Syphilis outbreak spread from Naples throughout Europe
Both events resulted from Columbus's voyage and its immediate consequences
Yeongsan-gun's accession in 1494 led to political instability and his deposition in 1506
